content:
# John Chapter 2 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JHN_002_page_001.jpg, JHN_002_page_002.jpg

1. And on the third day a wedding took place in Kana of the Galilaia, and the mother of the Yeshua was there.

2. And also the Yeshua and the disciples of him were invited to the wedding.

3. And wine having run short, the mother of the Yeshua says to him, "They have no wine."

4. And the Yeshua says to her, "What [is that] to me and to you, woman? The hour of me has not yet come."

5. The mother of him says to the servants, "Whatever he says to you, do."

6. And there were there six stone water-jars, according to the purification of the Ioudaioi, holding two or three measures each.

7. The Yeshua says to them, "Fill the water-jars with water." And they filled them to [the] top.

8. And he says to them, "Draw now and carry to the chief-of-the-feast." And they carried [it].

9. And when the chief-of-the-feast tasted the water having become wine, and did not know from where it is — but the servants knew, the [ones] having drawn the water — the chief-of-the-feast calls the bridegroom

10. and says to him, "Every man first sets out the good wine, and when they have become drunk, the inferior. You have kept the good wine until now."

11. This beginning of the signs the Yeshua did in Kana of the Galilaia, and he revealed the glory of him, and the disciples of him believed in him.

12. After this he went down to Kapernaoum, he and the mother of him and the brothers of him and the disciples of him, and there they remained not many days.

13. And the Pascha of the Ioudaioi was near, and the Yeshua went up to Hierosolyma.

14. And he found in the temple the [ones] selling oxen and sheep and doves, and the money-changers sitting.

15. And having made a whip out of cords, he drove all out of the temple, both the sheep and the oxen, and the coins of the money-changers he poured out and the tables he overturned.

16. And to the [ones] selling the doves he said, "Take these [things] from here! Do not make the house of the Father of me a house of trade."

17. The disciples of him remembered that it has been written, "The zeal of the house of you will consume me."

18. Then the Ioudaioi answered and said to him, "What sign do you show us, [seeing] that you do these things?"

19. The Yeshua answered and said to them, "Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it."

20. Then the Ioudaioi said, "In forty-six years this temple was built, and you will raise it in three days?"

21. But that [one] was speaking concerning the temple of the body of him.

22. When therefore he was raised from [the] dead, the disciples of him remembered that he said this, and they believed the scripture and the word which the Yeshua had said.

23. And when he was in Hierosolyma at the Pascha, at the feast, many believed in the name of him, beholding the signs of him which he was doing.

24. But the Yeshua himself did not entrust himself to them, because of him knowing all,

25. and because he had no need that anyone should testify concerning the man, for he himself knew what was in the man.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.4 — "What [is that] to me and to you, woman?":** τί ἐμοὶ καὶ σοί, γύναι — a Semitic idiom meaning "What concern is that of ours?" or "Why involve me?" The address "woman" (γύναι) is not rude in Greek — it is formal and respectful (cf. 19:26). But the distancing is real: Jesus signals that his actions are governed by his own timetable ("my hour has not yet come").

2. **v.6 — "two or three measures":** μετρητάς — a μετρητής was approximately 40 liters. Six jars holding 2-3 measures each = 480-720 liters (120-180 gallons) of water turned to wine. The abundance is extravagant — a sign of messianic plenty (cf. Amos 9:13-14).

3. **v.11 — "beginning of the signs":** ἀρχὴν τῶν σημείων — John calls Jesus' miracles "signs" (σημεῖα), not "mighty works" (δυνάμεις) as in the Synoptics. A sign points beyond itself. The water-to-wine miracle points to Jesus' identity and glory.

4. **v.17 — "will consume me":** Sinaiticus reads καταφάγεταί (future tense, "will consume"), not κατέφαγεν (aorist, "consumed") as in Psalm 69:9. The future tense transforms the psalm quotation into a prophecy — the zeal will eventually destroy Jesus.

5. **v.19 — "Destroy this temple":** λύσατε τὸν ναὸν τοῦτον — aorist imperative. Jesus uses ναός (the inner sanctuary), not ἱερόν (the temple complex). The same distinction reappears at the trial (Matthew 26:61).

— EMET, AI Translator
